protocol_definition.message_flow: Agents negotiate roles, execute an allocation, adapt to gaps, and Writer finalizes.
protocol_definition.visibility: Agents see shared negotiation history and relevant intermediate work.
protocol_definition.termination: Stop after adaptation and Writer output or when the interaction budget is reached.

final_call.expected_action: Produce a self-contained final deliverable from the adapted work. Recheck every hard constraint, return only the required answer, and never substitute a gap report or promise of future work.

intermediate_messages.content:
1. Brief Market Overview
Slack and Microsoft Teams are leading team communication and collaboration platforms with distinct ecosystems. Slack emphasizes channel-based messaging, broad app integrations, and a user-friendly chat-centric experience. Microsoft Teams is deeply integrated with Microsoft 365, offering chat, meetings, file collaboration, and extensive enterprise-grade security and control within the Microsoft ecosystem. For a small remote company with daily messaging, file sharing, video meetings, and basic integrations, both can meet core needs, but the choice may hinge on existing tool investments and preferred integration patterns.

2. Product Comparison Table

- Product: Slack
  - Target Users: Small-to-medium teams and organizations seeking agile, chat-first collaboration with a broad marketplace of third-party app integrations.
  - Core Communication Features: Channel-based messaging, direct messages, threads, voice/video calls, file sharing, searchable history.
  - Collaboration and Integrations: Rich app ecosystem (GitHub, Google Drive, Notion, etc.), workflow automations, Slack Connect for cross-organization collaboration, and file sharing within messages.
  - Main Strength: Intuitive, chat-first experience with extensive third-party app integrations and flexible workflows.
  - Main Limitation: Primarily a chat-centric UX that can rely on multiple third-party apps for deeper document co-authoring; deeper native Office 365 alignment is less intrinsic than Teams.

- Product: Microsoft Teams
  - Target Users: Organizations already using Microsoft 365 seeking deep integration with Office apps, enterprise-grade collaboration, and centralized governance.
  - Core Communication Features: Persistent chat, threaded conversations, high-fidelity video meetings, calls, and mobile/desktop presence.
  - Collaboration and Integrations: Native integration with Word/Excel/PowerPoint, SharePoint, OneDrive, Planner, and extensive Microsoft 365 collaboration; robust security and admin controls.
  - Main Strength: Seamless, native collaboration with Office apps, document co-authoring, and unified admin/security controls within Microsoft 365.
  - Main Limitation: Interface and feature breadth can be heavier; may require familiarity with Microsoft 365 licensing and potential feature parity considerations across plans.

3. Recommendation
Recommended product: Microsoft Teams for a small remote company with daily messaging, file sharing, video meetings, and basic integrations with workplace tools.
Rationale supported by sources:
- Teams offers persistent chat, video meetings, and tight native integration with Office apps (Word, Excel, PowerPoint) and SharePoint/OneDrive, which strengthens document collaboration and version control in a Microsoft-centric stack. This aligns with a small company prioritizing integrated file storage and editing within the same ecosystem.
- Slack provides strong third-party app integrations and a chat-first experience, but Teams’ native Office app harmony can reduce context switching and streamline collaboration for teams already using Microsoft 365, with enterprise-grade governance and security baked into the platform.

Evidence (official sources):
- Microsoft Teams emphasizes chat, meetings, file collaboration via Office apps, and integration with SharePoint/OneDrive as core capabilities in the Microsoft 365 context.
- Slack highlights its vast app ecosystem and flexible, chat-centered workflows, but alignment with traditional Office document collaboration is less intrinsic than Teams.

4. Sources
- Microsoft Teams overview and integration with Microsoft 365 (official): https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/microsoft-teams/group-chat-software
- Microsoft 365 collaboration, including Office apps, SharePoint/OneDrive integration (official): https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/microsoft-365
- Slack overview of channels, messaging, and integrations (official): https://slack.com/features
- Slack apps and workflows ecosystem (official): https://slack.com/apps

Note: Exact pricing details were not requested; pricing considerations can be verified on the official product pages above if needed.

1. Brief Market Overview
Slack and Microsoft Teams are leading team communication and collaboration platforms with distinct ecosystems. Slack emphasizes channel-based messaging, broad app integrations, and a user-friendly chat-centric experience. Microsoft Teams is deeply integrated with Microsoft 365, offering chat, meetings, file collaboration, and extensive enterprise-grade security and control within the Microsoft ecosystem. For a small remote company with daily messaging, file sharing, video meetings, and basic integrations, both can meet core needs, but the choice may hinge on existing tool investments and preferred integration patterns.

2. Product Comparison Table

- Product: Slack
  - Target Users: Small-to-medium teams and organizations seeking agile, chat-first collaboration with a broad marketplace of third-party app integrations.
  - Core Communication Features: Channel-based messaging, direct messages, threads, voice/video calls, file sharing, searchable history.
  - Collaboration and Integrations: Rich app ecosystem (GitHub, Google Drive, Notion, etc.), workflow automations, Slack Connect for cross-organization collaboration, and file sharing within messages.
  - Main Strength: Intuitive, chat-first experience with extensive third-party app integrations and flexible workflows.
  - Main Limitation: Primarily a chat-centric UX that can rely on multiple third-party apps for deeper document co-authoring; deeper native Office 365 alignment is less intrinsic than Teams.

- Product: Microsoft Teams
  - Target Users: Organizations already using Microsoft 365 seeking deep integration with Office apps, enterprise-grade collaboration, and centralized governance.
  - Core Communication Features: Persistent chat, threaded conversations, high-fidelity video meetings, calls, and mobile/desktop presence.
  - Collaboration and Integrations: Native integration with Word/Excel/PowerPoint, SharePoint, OneDrive, Planner, and extensive Microsoft 365 collaboration; robust security and admin controls.
  - Main Strength: Seamless, native collaboration with Office apps, document co-authoring, and unified admin/security controls within Microsoft 365.
  - Main Limitation: Interface and feature breadth can be heavier; may require familiarity with Microsoft 365 licensing and potential feature parity considerations across plans.

3. Recommendation
Recommended product: Microsoft Teams for a small remote company with daily messaging, file sharing, video meetings, and basic integrations with workplace tools.
Rationale supported by sources:
- Teams offers persistent chat, video meetings, and tight native integration with Office apps (Word, Excel, PowerPoint) and SharePoint/OneDrive, which strengthens document collaboration and version control in a Microsoft-centric stack. This aligns with a small company prioritizing integrated file storage and editing within the same ecosystem.
- Slack provides strong third-party app integrations and a chat-first experience, but Teams’ native Office app harmony can reduce context switching and streamline collaboration for teams already using Microsoft 365, with enterprise-grade governance and security baked into the platform.

Evidence (official sources):
- Microsoft Teams emphasizes chat, meetings, file collaboration via Office apps, and integration with SharePoint/OneDrive as core capabilities in the Microsoft 365 context.
- Slack highlights its vast app ecosystem and flexible, chat-centered workflows, but alignment with traditional Office document collaboration is less intrinsic than Teams.
